misconceptionSummary: Students think a longer decimal is always larger.

steps.narration: More digits after the decimal point does not mean a bigger number. What matters is which digit is in the most important column — the tenths column.
Compare the tenths column first. Nine tenths is more than eight tenths — the hundredths digit in 0.85 is irrelevant once tenths differ.
Adding a trailing zero makes both the same length without changing the value. Now you can compare digit by digit — ninety hundredths beats eighty-five hundredths.
Zero point nine. Fewer digits, bigger value. Length is never the measure of size for decimals.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: assuming 0.85 > 0.9 because 85 > 9. Decimals compare by column, not by overall digit length.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Don't compare decimals like integers. Look at the tenths column first — that's where size is decided.
